talk_metadata.talk_title: Genetic interactions in cancer: detection, modeling, and clinical relevance

key_data_gaps: No experimental validation of predicted epistatic modules
No single‑cell resolution to confirm co‑occurrence in the same cell

crispro_opportunity.description: Network modules predict patient survival and tumor subtype; CrisPRO could provide a computational framework to stratify patients by module membership for prognosis and trial enrichment.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "mutational profiles associated with network modules are informative of patient survival" and "early mutations in these pretumoral lesions can help predict future tumor subtypes".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate epistatic network signatures with clinical outcomes to generate robust, reproducible biomarker panels for subtype prediction.

cited_competitors.name: Sanger Institute
cited_competitors.context: Developer of the NDSCV software used in the analysis

cited_competitors.name: Solid Park
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned for work on genetic interactions between somatic mutations and copy number variation

unresolved_questions: How do the predicted epistatic modules perform in prospective clinical cohorts?
Can single‑cell sequencing validate that module‑specific mutations co‑occur within the same tumor cell?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Quantifying Epistasis and Evolutionary Trajectories in Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't have enough data for more than a few genes; the model grows exponentially while data grows linearly"
Speaker said: "We haven't looked at copy‑number effects yet, but there are papers on it"

key_data_gaps: Limited to pairwise analysis due to data sparsity
No incorporation of copy‑number or indel mutation types yet

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO’s ability to model higher‑order epistatic interactions could overcome the exponential scaling limitation highlighted by the speaker.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "With four genes it's very complicated… the data doesn’t grow exponentially".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s scalable probabilistic framework can jointly infer substitution rates for larger gene sets without exponential blow‑up.

crispro_opportunity.description: The method identifies synergistic driver pairs (e.g., KRAS‑KEAP1) that could be targeted with combination therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that KRAS synergistically enhances selection for KEAP1 and SDK11 mutations.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can prioritize drug combinations by quantifying the magnitude of synergistic selection coefficients.

cited_competitors.name: SigMutCel
cited_competitors.context: Tool mentioned for estimating mutation signatures used to derive neutral mutation rates

cited_competitors.name: BNDS
cited_competitors.context: Tool referenced for neutral mutation rate estimation

unresolved_questions: How robust are the inferred substitution rates when integrating copy‑number alterations?
Can the framework be extended to incorporate subclonal allele frequency information?

assets_to_track: Jorge Alfaro-Murillo’s epistasis inference software (Python implementation)

talk_metadata.talk_title: Tobacco Smoking and the Somatic Selective Epistasis in Lung Adenocarcinoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Smoking tends to increase the selection for KRAS, KEAP1 and SDK11"
Speaker said: "EGFR is not selected at all in smokers"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Reduced selection pressure for EGFR mutations in smokers diminishes TKI benefit

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Trials often do not stratify by smoking history, mixing ever‑smokers and never‑smokers
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Smoking status as a predictive biomarker for EGFR TKI response
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering of subgroup analyses for never‑smokers

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Efficacy is highly dependent on smoking‑related selection; without accounting for environment, the therapeutic window narrows
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing EGFR inhibitors without smoking‑status stratification may dilute clinical signal

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that EGFR mutations are less frequent in smokers (data) but then concluded that EGFR TKIs should be used uniformly, ignoring the reduced selection in smokers

key_data_gaps: No prospective trial data separating smokers vs non‑smokers for EGFR TKI efficacy
Limited functional validation of smoking‑specific epistatic interactions

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate smoking history into CrisPRO’s predictive models to refine EGFR‑targeted therapy selection.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "The substantially stronger selection for EGFR mutations in never smoker LUAD implies that EGFR TKIs should yield greater clinical benefit in never smokers".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can combine mutational signatures with environmental covariates (smoking) to generate a composite risk score for EGFR TKI response.

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify synergistic driver pairs (e.g., KRAS‑KEAP1) prevalent in smokers for combination therapy design.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that KRAS and KEAP1 have stronger selection in ever‑smokers and co‑mutation worsens survival.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can rank combination candidates by quantifying joint selection coefficients in smoker‑specific cohorts.

cited_competitors.name: KRAS inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as more selected in smokers

unresolved_questions: Do other environmental factors (e.g., air pollution) similarly reshape epistatic landscapes?
Can longitudinal sampling track how smoking‑induced epistasis evolves during therapy?
